content.text: TipSome modern compilers may give an error about having virtual functions and an accessible non-virtual destructor. If this is the case, add a virtual destructor to the base class. In the above program, add this to the definition ofBase:virtual ~Base() = default;We discuss virtual destructors in lesson25.4 -- Virtual destructors, virtual assignment, and overriding virtualization.

Some modern compilers may give an error about having virtual functions and an accessible non-virtual destructor. If this is the case, add a virtual destructor to the base class. content.code: rBase is a C

C c{};
std::cout << c.getName(); // will always call C::getName

A a { c }; // copies the A portion of c into a (don't do this)
std::cout << a.getName(); // will always call A::getName

content.text: Key insightVirtual function resolution only works when a member function is called through a pointer or reference to a class type object.
Virtual function resolution only works when a member function is called through a pointer or reference to a class type object.

content.text: RuleIf a function is virtual, all matching overrides in derived classes are implicitly virtual.
If a function is virtual, all matching overrides in derived classes are implicitly virtual.
Best practiceNever call virtual functions from constructors or destructors.
Never call virtual functions from constructors or destructors.

list: What do the following programs print? This exercise is meant to be done by inspection, not by compiling the examples with your compiler.

content.text: Show SolutionA. Another tricky one. When we create a C object, the A part is constructed first. When the A constructor is called to do this, it calls virtual function getName(). Because the B and C parts of the class aren’t set up yet, this resolves to A::getName().Next lesson25.3The override and final specifiers, and covariant return typesBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson25.1Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects
